operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Installing and Building OpenCog (14e4ad143c9407da)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Installing and Building OpenCog
Messages: 5
Date range: 1 Jul 2015 to 9 Jul 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e4ad143c9407da

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: Gavriel Loria
Date: 1 Jul 2015

The purpose of this post is to offer general instructions for installing and building OpenCog. First, prepare a main opencog directory and git clone opencog, atomspace, and cogutils. Then install them in order: cogutils, atomspace, opencog. Each follows: mkdir build, cd build, cmake .. [...]

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 2 Jul 2015

If there is anything missing from http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Building_OpenCog and the Noobs page please don't hesitate to edit as you see fit.

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Gavriel Loria
Date: 3 Jul 2015

Okay, I added these instructions under a label 'New Instructions' in the Noobs page.

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-10

Yeah, Gavriel is probably too new to be editing the how-to-build opencog pages; but they should be changed to reflect the new github repo structure...

--linas

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 9 Jul 2015

@Gavriel thank for the new instructions. @Linas, I have reviewed the section added by Gavriel, it is informative enough. Cleaning up and expanding the noobs pages is on my todo list.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Intention, Emotional Centers, Empathy and finally Language Understanding (1511934824033b3b)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Intention, Emotional Centers, Empathy and finally Language Understanding
Messages: 2
Date range: 17 Nov 2015 to 2015-11-18
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1511934824033b3b

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Blaine Whiteley
Date: 17 Nov 2015

Hi Guys, I'd love to get involved from the standpoint of understanding an external Agent's intention. I feel an AGI needs an awareness of diminished capabilities and 'pain' grounded in actual occurrence before any true understanding of intentional actions could occur.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-18

Hi, The framework we're using in OpenCog to deal with motivated action selection (and emotion) is called OpenPsi, a modification of Joscha Bach's Psi / MicroPsi model. Amen Belayneh is currently refactoring the prior OpenPsi code for use with a Hanson robot head, dialogue system and Minecraft character.

ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Interfacing DeSTIN to OpenCOG (14e83f21935535a7)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Interfacing DeSTIN to OpenCOG
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-07-12 to 2015-07-13
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e83f21935535a7

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: Benjamin Menkuec
Date: 2015-07-12

I thought about the interface of DeSTIN and OpenCog. My favorite theory how this linking is done biologically is consciousness being different parts of the brain synchronized through long axon fibers. [...]

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-12

This makes sense conceptually. The challenge at the moment is getting the intermediate and upper layers of DeSTIN to have nodes with meaningful patterns in them, which are worth feeding into OpenCog. We are currently looking at hybrid architectures combining aspects of DeSTIN with aspects of denoising stacked autoencoders or convolutional autoencoders.

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Benjamin Menkuec
Date: 2015-07-13

I think cognitive synergy is very important when training the vision network, because biologically most of the training is done supervised through other cognitive modalities. A joint training of more than one modality would be a good idea, most practicably vision + language.

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-13

Actually what I am thinking about now is related but not the same as the wiki page, see attached StackedSemilocalizedAutoencoders.pdf.

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-13

I agree that cross-modal information will be very important for AGI vision processing. However, I also think one can make a first-pass vision-cognition interface without it. It will just make dumb mistakes sometimes, when cross-modal information would help it.

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: Benjamin Menkuec
Date: 2015-07-13

I think the cross-modal information has 2 purposes: 1. supervised training, 2. assisting the recognition process. For purpose 2 it would be dispensable and just lead to some stupid errors. For 1 I am not so sure.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Internship (14ba2e530e568d1a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Internship
Messages: 3
Date range: 19 Feb 2015 to 20 Feb 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ba2e530e568d1a

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Roman Treutlein
Date: 19 Feb 2015

Hello, I am a student of Cognitive Science at the University of Osnabrueck. Since I am interested in AI and have been following this project, I thought it might be possible for me to work as an intern on Opencog. Starting around September for 3-6 months.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-02-20

Hi Roman,

Hmmm, Here in Hong Kong we don't have any current source of funding to sponsor internships. If your university provides funding for your study then we can potentially provide a desk and computer for you and some training and collaboration ;)

-- Ben

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Roman Treutlein
Date: 20 Feb 2015

Hi Ben, I will have to check with my University if this work is close enough to our studies, but that should not be a problem. Paying for my stay shouldn't be a problem either.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Introduction ) (1500ebccfae7da01)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Introduction )
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-09-27 to 2015-09-30
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1500ebccfae7da01

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: Richhiey Thomas
Date: 2015-09-27

I am a third year computer engineering undergrad from India. I recently came across this project and having an affinity towards machine learning and AI, I would like to be a contributor.

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: Rohit Shinde
Date: 2015-09-27

There are a couple of issues you can work on at github.com/opencog/opencog/issues. Read up on the atomspace. Follow the opencog tutorial.

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Jan Matusiewicz
Date: 2015-09-28

About the Minecraft interface task - it requires also writing down rules of the Minecraft world in the form of AtomSpace rules, right? Or can some part of the knowledge be learned by experience?

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-09-29

In principle those rules can be learned via experience, though getting that to happen will require some non-trivial fiddling with the OpenCog learning/reasoning algorithms. The plumbing for connecting Minecraft to OpenCog is pretty much done by Yi-Shan and Brad this last summer.

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Jan Matusiewicz
Date: 2015-09-29

Do you feel the need for changing these learning and reasoning algorithms, so that OpenCog requires only attaching to raw API of virtual world like Minecraft?

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-09-30

It is not so much matter of changing the algorithms themselves, as of changing the way the algorithms are connected to the input and output aspect of OpenCog. The work Amen is now doing with OpenPsi will provide one way of connecting the learning/reasoning algorithm to the I/O.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Is there a completed definition for the knowledge representation model of ope... (14ae683ee6bc06a4)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Is there a completed definition for the knowledge representation model of ope...
Messages: 7
Date range: 2015-01-13 to 2015-01-14
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ae683ee6bc06a4

============================================================

--- Message 1/7 ---
From: shangfenghuresearch@gmail.com
Date: 2015-01-13

I am considering to write a paper for AGI2015. OpenCog may be one of the related works. I want to compare the knowledge representation models but I did not find the completed model definition for opencog in the publications.

--- Message 2/7 ---
From: shangfenghuresearch@gmail.com
Date: 2015-01-13

Is the knowledge representation model Probabilistic Logic Network?

--- Message 3/7 ---
From: Dustin Ezell
Date: 2015-01-14

The AtomSpace is the knowledge store which is a generalized hypergraph. PLN does reasoning on the AtomSpace.

--- Message 4/7 ---
From: shangfenghuresearch@gmail.com
Date: 2015-01-13

Is there a completed definition for AtomSpace? What are the relations between the nodes? Is a knowledge base of AtomSpace built from Natural Language Material or structured data?

--- Message 5/7 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-01-14

You would have to read one of Ben's books to get the full answer. There are 3 or 4 or 5 books on opencog now, with the newest being the most accurate. The wiki also contains a lot of information, but it's not complete.

--- Message 6/7 ---
From: Dustin Ezell
Date: 2015-01-14

There is a preprint draft of his latest book at goertzel.org/monkeyburger/bbm_main.pdf. Check out section IV.

--- Message 7/7 ---
From: shangfenghuresearch@gmail.com
Date: 2015-01-14

Dustin, Thanks. It's really helpful.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Like to contribute to MOSES (14c89e56e2feab48)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Like to contribute to MOSES
Messages: 4
Date range: 5 Apr 2015 to 2015-04-06
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c89e56e2feab48

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Tapiawala Sahil
Date: 5 Apr 2015

Hi,
I had come across opencog while I was planning to apply for GSOC, though I didn't apply, I continue to read the blog and have found the project really interesting and would like to contribute. On the GSoC 2015 ideas page, there was a mention of modification of the hBOA algorithm to better MOSES. [...]

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-04-05

Hi Tapiwala,

Google Groups has been acting weird lately, I don't know why. If you tell me your email address I'll try to get the Google Group to accept it, sure...

About MOSES, to work on modifying/improving the underlying algorithm doesn't require a very deep knowledge of AI, what it requires is

-- reasonably strong C++ knowledge/ability
-- ability to read papers and understand the algorithms described therein

thx
ben

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Sahil Tapiawala
Date: 6 Apr 2015

Hi, I think I am quite proficient in coding and wouldn't have a problem analysing the algorithms. Could you direct me to the resources required to get started?

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-04-06

Hi Sahil,

MOSES is here: https://github.com/opencog/opencog/tree/master/opencog/learning/moses
and the learning algorithms are here: https://github.com/opencog/opencog/tree/master/opencog/learning/moses/optimization

A good place to start might be to continue the work Linas started a while back on integrating Boosting with MOSES. MOSES-Boosting hasn't been tested systematically on supervised categorization problems.

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Linking craze (14e03626c8acd6b1)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Linking craze
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-06-17 to 2015-06-18
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e03626c8acd6b1

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-06-17

Hi,

I'm stuck with a linking error, can't run-time link to Instantiator::instantiate. You may reproduce it as follows

1. Get my branch
git clone git@github.com:ngeiswei/opencog.git
git branch move-pln-utest-from-atomspace-repo

2. Run the PLNRulesUTest
mkdir build; cd build; cmake ..
cd tests/reasoning; make -j4 PLNRulesUTest && ./PLNRulesUTest

3. Observe the result
./PLNRulesUTest: symbol lookup error: /usr/local/lib/opencog/libquery.so: undefined symbol

Using the nm tool I can see that on my system Instantiator::instantiate is undefined in libquery.so but is defined in libexecution.so.

Any suggestion is welcome, thanks,
Nil

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: mandeep bhatia
Date: 2015-06-18

Hi,
 It might be the same function name with different signature or namespace is required. Usually this is one of the problems with c++ doing name mangling while compiling code into library that its not fully visible where the problem is.

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-06-18

Thanks mandeep, but I don't think it's a problem of different signature or namespace, Instantiator::instantiate is only defined once in atomspace/opencog/atoms/execution/Instantiator.{h,cc}.

Last remark, according to ldd, libquery.so doesn't show a dependence on libexecution.so, but libexecution shows it on libquery.so. There is a cyclic dependence that must be resolved at the linking level.

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-18

I think I know what this is, and I don't really know how to solve it. This is a dragon that visits more and more often.

There are circular dependencies absolutely everywhere: instantiate depends on both python-eval and on scheme-eval. both python and scheme depend on the atomspace. The atomspace depends on various atoms. BindLink depends on query, which depends on the atomspace...

The only 'solution' I found is to make even smaller shared libraries which hold code that does not depend on anything.

--linas

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-18

Interestingly though not helpfully in the immediate term, this guy suggests extending cmake to manage complex dependencies by formulating them collective as a Boolean satisfaction problem ;D

https://meetingcpp.com/tl_files/2014/talks/DependencyHell.pdf

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-06-18

The error doesn't occur on my desktop, only on my laptop, on it other errors occur like on a fresh install, boost isn't found anymore. Something fishy is going on with my laptop.

Nil

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Looking to contribute in the hardware end (14cf956b1fd38151)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Looking to contribute in the hardware end
Messages: 11
Date range: 2015-04-26 to 2015-05-10
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14cf956b1fd38151

============================================================

--- Message 1/11 ---
From: Surya Banerjee
Date: 2015-04-26

I am an Electronics Engineering student, very interested in intelligent embedded systems. I would love to contribute in the hardware end of OpenCog.

--- Message 2/11 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-03

Mandeep is working on robosapien. Hanson Robotics is strongly affiliated with OpenCog, so all the code in github.com/hansonrobotics is open source. There has not yet been any particularly strong hardware side to opencog yet.

--- Message 3/11 ---
From: mandeep bhatia
Date: 2015-05-04

On the hardware side we are developing a low cost open source robot based on Robosapien toy for navigation and interaction using vision and voice.

--- Message 4/11 ---
From: Sahil Tapiawala
Date: 2015-05-08

Following this thread for any updates on the Hardware implementation.

--- Message 5/11 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-08

Wow! More hardware people! I hope something starts happening!

--- Message 6/11 ---
From: Surya Banerjee
Date: 2015-05-08

I am relatively new to OpenCog and AI. Could someone point to the right place to start learning for hardware implementation?

--- Message 7/11 ---
From: mandeep bhatia
Date: 2015-05-09

We are interested in developing hardware for real world goals and AI processing. Current robot has a ROS stack. You should become familiar with ROS.

--- Message 8/11 ---
From: Mahesh Kommareddi
Date: 2015-05-09

I was under the impression that those talking about hardware are getting at implementing processing in hardware. Like FPGAs?

--- Message 9/11 ---
From: mandeep bhatia
Date: 2015-05-10

On the hardware front we are not discussing compute hardware but mostly robot circuitry.

--- Message 10/11 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-09

I have just barely started trying to hookup opencog to ROS. Almost nothing works yet, but I should have an interesting demo in a few days. My goal is a rudimentary state machine to control Eva.

--- Message 11/11 ---
From: mandeep bhatia
Date: 2015-05-10

I am near completing stuff for itf. After that I hope I can contribute on the perception side.

Hello Everyone, I am Mubashir Hanif, currently studying Computer Science at NUCES-FAST Karachi. I have expertise in C/C++, Java, matlab and fairly good experience in machine learning and Image Processing.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Make atom Cplusplus factory better (150f0f6022c95f98)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Make atom Cplusplus factory better
Messages: 4
Date range: 2015-11-10 to 2015-11-11
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150f0f6022c95f98

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-10

While debugging and navigating the AtomSpace code I wonder if we could simplify all that. What about we auto generate a C++ hierarchy of nodes and links following atom_types.script, filling the blanks with default constructors and keeping existing code in opencog/atoms. The auto generation script could also verify consistency with atom_types.script.

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-10

It would also make C++ hypergraph definition terser: ListLink(ConceptNode("A"), PredicateNode("P")) instead of as->add_link(...)

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-11

I don't think I really like that idea very much. It is a premature design optimization; the code is still very highly experimental, and I don't want to bake in what might be a bad design decision. Experimental: e.g. after discussions with Ben, its clear we should get rid of TV and AV. But how? I tried to create ProtoAtoms but there are implementation problems. Adding auto-generated code would make this harder. Its solving a non-problem. Its not like the current code is hard to maintain.

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-11

You can do this today with macros. Some unit test cases already do: #define AL as->add_link, #define AN as->add_node. With some template magic you could clean that up. But I discourage people from doing too much atomese in C++ anyway.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Managing general Atom properties ... was Re Recording global count values... (14da3355a4a86d71)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Managing general Atom properties ... was Re Recording global count values...
Messages: 15
Date range: 2015-05-30 to 2015-06-10
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14da3355a4a86d71

============================================================

--- Message 1/15 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-30

OK, now this thread (split off from a previous one) is about how to
store and manage more general Atom properties....  (I want to split
this off from the SimpleCountTV topic, which can be handled by
specialized "hacks"...)

The current situation is that

-- certain properties like TruthValue, AttentionValue are wired into
the Atom class

-- if we want to associate other properties with Atoms, we don't have
a clear way to do this.   We can add new TruthValue or AttentionValue
types with additional fields in them, but this becomes confusing ...
we get sort of a maze of truth value types with overlapping fields,
etc.

The current situation is workable but leads to some mess...

So it would be nice to have a more general, flexible, yet efficient
 [...]

--- Message 2/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-30

There's some points in this email that I strongly disagree with, but I'm
going to just ignore those.  Let me strongly agree that the current TV and
AV design leaves much to be desired.

Re: the Get and Put links -- they are perfectly ordinary atoms, just like
any others. They have no magic properties of any kind, with regard to the
atomspace.  There is merely a chunk of code -- cog-execute! that interprets
them in a special way: it knows how to "execute" them, to generate other
atoms (which are also perfectly ordinary).

So...

?? Well .. the above statement is  strange and confusing to me. You can add
extra atom properties just fine, without using GetLink an PutLink at all.
...

But you can also just say it direc...

--- Message 3/15 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 30 May 2015

It might be off topic, but would you mind sharing this vision and why?

Just curious

--- Message 4/15 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-06-01

Hi,

regarding AtomSpace API changes, sure it can rapidly get daunting, but 
here, as far as SimpleCountTV is concerned, it's only an additive 
change, right? So it virtually shouldn't affect the existing code.

The idea of computing P=n/N on the fly and updating n or N when 
necessary seems soooooo fundamental, that it would make sense to me to 
have it in the API (actually, I'm not sure, but it seems it sorta used 
to be in the AtomSpace, via the version TVs, not sure at all, as at that 
time I didn't understand the AtomSpace API much).

As for associating general quantities (or other types) to an Atom, using 
EvaluationLink/ExecutionOutputLink/PutLink/GetLink seems perfectly 
adequate to me, and if the AtomSpace is too slow it should be sped up 
 [...]

--- Message 5/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-04

On Mon, Jun 1, 2015 at 2:10 AM, Nil Geisweiller <ngeiswei@googlemail.com>
wrote:

yes, no and sort-of.  Yes, the notion of a frequency is central (by
frequency, I mean the fraction n/N )

however, we have not concluded .. we have barely started ... the
conversation on the best way to encode bayesian probabilities (and, by
extension, bayesian nets and markov nets) in the atomspace.  You started
that in an email chain a month or two ago, but it never concluded: it uses
ContextLinks to store conditional probablities.  I wrote some prototypes,
but feel like I only just got started.

the details of this need to be worked out in greater detail, including
practical examples.  Exactly how it fits with PLN, and just where it
clashes with PLN, is not clear, at all.

There is also design overlap wi.. [...]

--- Message 6/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-04

Hi Amen,

I was going to write a long reply, but ran out of time.

I can outline day-dreams ... so, the atomspace is like the DataLog language
-- a subset of prolog, but storing floating-point TV's.  What can we do to
make the atomspace look more like prolog+TV's?

What are TV's anyway? There's PLN, there's Bayes,, there is Markov, they
are all inter-related, how can we allow all of these to co-exist and work
well, complete with example demos/programs?

Unlike prolog, the atomspace has types.  Well, prolog has types, but they
are implicit, rather than explicit. The famous typed languages are ML, CaML
and Haskell. What ideas can we steal from these languages? But Haskell is
almost the complete opposite of DataLog, because of its no-side-effects
philosophy. [...]

--- Message 7/15 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 6 Jun 2015

I imagine many folks are using key-value stores outside of Atomspace for 
very those purposes.  I currently use Python dicts, to store data keyed  by 
Atom handle.  Simple, flexible, and fast.  Downside: it's "private" within 
my own code and therefore can't be used by other code in pursuit of 
 [...]

--- Message 8/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-06

Hi Jim,

Any kind  of information storage that exists outside of the atomspace is a
burden:
-- it cannot be shared between multiple cogservers
-- it cannot be saved and restored, when rebooting a system
-- it cannot be searched by the pattern matcher
-- the API's (scheme, python) would need to be extended
-- it would need to be made thread-safe

We already have a completely general key-value store already, its called
the atomspace. To create a second key-value store outside of the atomspace
makes no sense.

What you seem to want is some sort of easier-to-use, more familiar way of
working with it.  I'm guessing that the unfinished DefineLink might be what
you want.  For example:

   DefineLink
        NameNode "some key"
        value...

 [...]

--- Message 9/15 ---
From: Cassio Pennachin
Date: 2015-06-08

Hi,

Any kind  of information storage that exists outside of the atomspace is a

It looks like these burdens could be solved by a well designed "plugin"
sytem for the AtomSpace, through which one could register information
containers and provide the generic infrastructure for sharing, saving,
loadin...

--- Message 10/15 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-08

Yeah -- I guess Linas's point is that, if we did have a plug-in
architecture, the API that a plug-in would have to fulfill would be
fairly complex...

For instance, we will soon be able to back up the Atomspace in both
postgres (already works) and Neo4j (a GSoC student is working on
it)....   So then a plugin container would need special code for
making it backuppable in both postgres and Neo4j

This sort of issue, multiplied over multiple aspect, may be a
sufficient argument for " replicating the domain-appropriate data
structure in the hypergraph" in many cases... this needs more
thinking...

-- Ben

--- Message 11/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-08

On Mon, Jun 8, 2015 at 1:54 AM, Cassio Pennachin <pennachin@gmail.com>
wrote:

I don't think it is justified under any circumstances whatsoever. Its like
going to Intel, and saying "gee I don't like your instruction set, I want
to have my own opcodes" ...  well, sure, of course you can, you can write a
trap for your own private opcodes and emulate any instruction set you
wish.  A famous one was "startio" which was a single instruction that would
trigger an absolutely huge device driver to do a DMA in the background.
Just a single instruction!  it was awesome!

The point is that almost no one does this (anymore) because its a dumb idea.

Either the atomspace provides the things that you need, or it doesn't, and
should be fixed.  To sit there and claim you want a plugin extensibility
archit. [...]

--- Message 12/15 ---
From: Cassio Pennachin
Date: 2015-06-09

Hi,

I don't think it is justified under any circumstances whatsoever.

You certainly know the Atomspace a million times better than I do. Back ten
years ago, it was easily justifiable to add special containers for temporal
and spatial data because the performance and memory savings were huge
compar...

--- Message 13/15 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-09

Here is my current "gut feel" about using the Atomspace in place of
the tentatively-proposed auxiliary containers...

I think that, for most of the purposes that we have now (or I envision
having in the near term), the Atomspace performance for putting and
getting Atoms is going to be OK... it's been sped up a lot since the
old days...

However, I suspect (but don't know for sure) that if we have an
application that's going to rapidly create and then "ask to be
forgotten" a lot of Atoms, then we'll probably end up with a
performance problem.   For instance -- this is a contrived example --
but suppose we had a lot of Atoms like

AtLocationLink
   LocationNode "Earth: 32.444, 77.666"
   ContextAnchorNode "123"

EmbeddedTruthValueLink <s,c>
   ContextAnchorNode "123"
   EvaluationLink
 [...]

--- Message 14/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-10

Hmm. Well, maybe I was just a bit too strong.

-- the atomspace performance is hardly stellar, and I wouldn't recommend it
for ...  any data that has an extremely regular pattern, is changing
rapidly, and does not need the things that the atomspace provides (assigns
TV's, applies PLN, does pattern matching) Its best suited for ad-hoc highly
irregular data.

-- over the years, I've observed a tendency to over-engineer, over-design
system components that are well-understood or are easy to understand, and a
tendency to neglect anything that is hard or confusing.

In this particular case, we have a system that almost no one uses, (the
atomspace), for which there are almost no apps, and the few apps that there
are have fallen into disrepair.  It seems wrong-headed to devote energy
 [...]

--- Message 15/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-10

For the "contrived example" below (and its a good one), let me ask: why do
you feel you need to put the data into the atomspace in the first place?
Are you going to run the rule engine on that data? Do some PLN reasoning on
it?

I like the example, because I've been contemplating doing something
similar, but for the location of the visible human faces that Eva is
looking at.   I can tell you why I want it in the atomspace: I want to
apply a long sequence of complex rules that will determine what sort of
emotions Eva will show on her face.

Should I also place the frame-by-frame 3D spatial location data in there?
Probably not. Eva's emotional response is probably not going to depend much
on the location.  It seems very unlikely that we'll do PLN inference on it.
 [...]

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Markov chain demo syntax (14d31b926c52515f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Markov chain demo syntax
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-05-07 to 2015-05-12
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14d31b926c52515f

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-05-07

I was looking through the Markov chain demo. Some questions occurred about the atomspace syntax. It seems the idea here is to use a ListLink to define a pair (variable, value) using an AnchorNode.

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-08

No particular reason. I avoided them because I did not want to collide with PLN or other subsystems. I picked the AnchorNode because its just different from everything else, no risk of confusion. I plan to start sticking FSMs into the atomspace, to do robot behaviors, and if PLN starts deducing unexpected things and garbaging up the FSM state, that would be bad.

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-05-07

It might be helpful to share a common notation between these different systems.

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-08

The anchor was invented as a parallel/concurrent-processing rendezvous point. When one process finishes some work, it drops off its result by attaching it to an anchor. Then, other agents can look for new work attached to anchor, then detach it, and do their processing on it. In an actor model, the sender attaches things to an anchor, the receiver unhooks from it.

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-09

The semantics of SubsetLink aka ExtensionalInheritanceLink is that the strength of the link is EXACTLY a conditional probability. PLN comes with specific ways of measuring confidence. We have discussed bifurcating SimpleTruthValue into SimpleFuzzyTruthValue and SimpleProbabilisticTruthValue.

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-09

My concern is having two different algorithms modifying the contents of the atomspace at the same time. If one algo is PLN, and another is performing specific TV calculations, then one may create TVs that are invalid from the other point of view.

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-11

Having multiple algorithms modifying the Atomspace at the same time is exactly what OpenCog is intended for... However, it needs to be done thoughtfully. I suggest we think through how to make these algorithms work together.

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-05-12

That is why the semantics of things should be generally coherent with PLN. Once we have the type system supporting monads we can easily differentiate pure vs side-effect structures.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Match only hypergraphs without variable (150dced829c63979)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Match only hypergraphs without variable
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-11-06 to 2015-11-09
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150dced829c63979

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-06

Is there a way to write a PM query that would only match sub-hypergraphs that are fully grounded? That is that do not contain any variable. The only way I can think of is using a GroundedPredicate no-variable function.

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-06

It works! If you know a better way, I am still interested. We probably want to have this no-variable function directly coded in the C++ API atomspace util.

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Misgana Bayetta
Date: 2015-11-06

Have you looked into AtomUtils.cc?

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Misgana Bayetta
Date: 2015-11-06

Never mind, the function that I pointed does something totally different.

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-06

There is not what I am looking for. For now I will code it in scheme.

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-09

I don't entirely get it. You want to find all graphs in the atomspace that have no variables in them? Not something I thought much before. Yeah, that pattern would work. The only other way would be to write some kind of C++ code that did the work directly.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Merging TV (150dc9a157682be2)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Merging TV
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-11-06 to 2015-11-09
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150dc9a157682be2

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-06

Hi, how do you guys have merged TVs so far? The scheme binding doesn't seem to contain any merging function. Currently a PLN formula just overwrites the existing TV, I don't think that's good.

Nil

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-09

merging has been a mess, mostly because the merge rules for fuzzy TV are different than the merge rules for probabilistic TV. Both of these are different from what one needs to do if maintaining a count. So the practice has been that everyone just computes exactly the TV they need for a given case.

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+MindAgents or not MindAgents (14aeda2246d1a5bb)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: MindAgents or not MindAgents
Messages: 4
Date range: 15 Jan 2015 to 2015-01-15
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14aeda2246d1a5bb

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 15 Jan 2015

In Ben's book the term MindAgent appears 252 times and seems conceptually core. On the other hand, Linas recently discouraged spending time on them. So what's the bottom line? Are Ben's conceptual MindAgents functionally different from MindAgents as implemented? Should we avoid using them?

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Keyvan Mir Mohammad Sadeghi
Date: 2015-01-15

Simply put, a MindAgent is the unit responsible for a specific cognitive task. What Linas pointed out was the way MindAgents are currently implemented in OpenCog. The book covers the design, not implementation details.

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-01-15

Yes exactly. A process that talked to the atomspace via zmq or rest or ros would also be a 'mind agent', in the abstract sense. --linas

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-15

Yeah, I think we all agree on this. The concept of a MindAgent as a piece of code encapsulating a cognitive process which does its work via interaction with the Atomspace --- this is key to the OpenCog design. However, the current specific implementation via round-robin scheduler is not critical to the design.

I do think the notion of a 'cognitive cycle' makes sense. However, I don't think it's good to require every cognitive process to operate within such a cycle.

-- Ben
